top10.introHtml: Ranked for the cheap fun second car buyer near Media, PA — prioritizing working top condition, model reliability, and driving character over raw mileage numbers. All picks are within 35 miles of ZIP 19063, $7k–$15k.

picks.whyGood: The Miata NC (2006–2015) is the most reliable drop-top you can buy at this price. Grand Touring spec adds Bilstein shocks, leather, heated seats, and the better-sorted suspension tune. At 177k miles this engine hasn't hit its limit — the MZR 2.0 routinely runs 200k+ with oil changes. 'Great Deal' rating, 20 miles away, dealer sale with some accountability.
picks.whatToVerify: Run top through a full open/close cycle — listen for motor hesitation or binding. Check headliner and carpet for water stains (rear seam is the leak point). Inspect the rear plastic window for yellowing or delamination. Look at rocker panels underneath for surface rust. Verify timing chain (no belt — chain is quiet with no rattle on cold start). Pull service records or at least check for Carfax oil-change history.
picks.bottomLine: Best fun per dollar in the search — take it to an independent Mazda-friendly shop for a $150 PPI and buy if it passes.

picks.whyGood: The Z3 2.8 has BMW's M52 inline-six — smooth, rev-happy, and far more characterful than the 4-cylinder versions. At $7,940 and 75k miles this is the cheapest truly sporting roadster in the search. 15 miles away. The M52 is known durable; most issues are maintenance-related, not structural.
picks.whatToVerify: Confirm it's a manual — auto Z3 is far less fun and harder to sell. Check coolant expansion tank for cracks (they split — $30 to replace). Inspect rocker panels and rear wheel arches for rust, common on PA/NJ cars. Run the soft top through a full cycle; hydraulic top motors are expensive if dead. Check for oil leaks at valve cover and rear main seal.
picks.bottomLine: Lowest-price inline-six roadster in the area — great entry point if the top works and it's a stick.

picks.whyGood: The E88 128i is the last BMW designed around driver engagement before cost-cutting took over — rear-wheel drive, inline-six (N52), light weight. At $9,995 with 74k miles and a 'Great Deal' rating from a reputable Ford dealer, this is a smart buy. Ford dealers inspect trades carefully and won't stake their reputation on a lemon.
picks.whatToVerify: Top must open and close fully — test both directions, listen for the hydraulic pump. Check rear window condition (plastic yellows). Verify HPFP (high-pressure fuel pump) history — recall-covered on many N52s. Look for oil leaks at valve cover gasket. Test headliner for water staining.
picks.bottomLine: Modern BMW roadster at a used-Miata price — the sweet spot of reliability, fun, and value.

picks.whyGood: 44,000 miles on a 1994 SN95 is genuinely exceptional — this is a low-use stored car. The 5.0L HO pushrod V8 (last year of the 302) is simple, proven, and sonorous. Private seller = negotiating room. Clean title verified on Cars.com.
picks.whatToVerify: SN95 convertible top frame rots at the mount-to-rocker junction — look hard at the A-pillar base and rocker seams with a magnet. Check the soft top for delamination and rear window crazing. Inspect the T-5 transmission for third-gear grinding (slow-shift to 3rd to test). Fuel lines should be replaced at this age regardless of mileage.
picks.bottomLine: If the frame is solid, 44k miles on a 5.0 Mustang GT at $10,500 is the deal of the search.

picks.whyGood: Mid-engine, true sports car feel at $11,250. 74k miles is moderate for a Boxster. West Chester, 11 miles away. The 986 Boxster is undervalued — the driving dynamics are genuinely special and parts supply has improved dramatically.
picks.whatToVerify: IMS bearing is the critical question — ask for any service records showing IMS replacement or check for LN Engineering IMS retrofit. Inspect underneath for RMS (rear main seal) oil weeping on the underside of the bell housing. Check coolant level and color (milky = head gasket from overheating). Run the top fully. Budget $1,200–1,500 for pre-emptive IMS fix if undocumented.
picks.bottomLine: Best driving machine in the list — but only buy with IMS documentation or budget for the fix upfront.

picks.whyGood: Newer model year (2012) and lower miles (58k) than the $9,995 pick above. 'High Demand' signals it may not last long. If you want a turn-key, newer BMW with less worry about age-related maintenance, spend the extra $4k here.
picks.whatToVerify: Same checklist as the 2011 128i — top cycle, rear window, HPFP, oil leaks. At this price point, insist on a PPI from an independent BMW shop before purchase.
picks.bottomLine: Pay $4k more vs. Rank 3 for 16k fewer miles and a newer model year — worthwhile if you want the cleaner car.

picks.whyGood: 27,598 miles on a 2002 Mercedes V8 cabriolet is extraordinary — this car was clearly garage-kept and rarely driven. The W208 CLK 430 with the 4.3L V8 is a genuinely elegant convertible. Clean title, no accidents.
picks.whatToVerify: Low mileage ≠ no problems on a 23-year-old car. Replace all rubber regardless: tires (age-cracked), belts, coolant hoses. The soft top is original at this age — have a top shop assess it. Check electrics carefully (window regulators, top motor, central locking — W208 electrical gremlins are real). Verify the engine hasn't sat long without running (fuel lines, injectors).
picks.bottomLine: A V8 cabriolet time capsule — stunning if the rubber and electrics check out, a headache if they don't.

picks.whyGood: The MR2 Spyder is a genuine mid-engine two-seater — this is the rare surprise of the search. 56k miles, 16 miles away, 'Great Deal' rating. The 1ZZ-FE engine is reliable, light, and rev-happy. If the SMT sequential transmission is converted or it's a manual, this is a genuinely special find.
picks.whatToVerify: Verify transmission type: if it has the SMT (Sequential Manual Transmission), ask specifically about clutch actuator condition — SMT actuators are expensive and parts are scarce. Manual is strongly preferred. Check top mechanism (it's a soft-top targa, not a full convertible — verify latch operation). Look for rust around the rear wheel arches. Engine oil leaks from the cam cover are common.
picks.bottomLine: Rare, mid-engine, fun — the most interesting car in the search if it's a stick.

picks.whyGood: The R52 MINI Cooper S with the supercharged 1.6L is one of the most characterful small convertibles ever built. At $8,452 with 65k miles and a 'Great Deal' rating, it's properly priced. MINI converts are surprisingly roomy for two people and the S model is legitimately quick.
picks.whatToVerify: Supercharger belt condition (listen for squealing on startup). Cold-start timing chain rattle is common — let the car warm up and listen. Check power steering hose for seeping at the pump fitting (very common). Inspect the top for tears and verify the folding mechanism works smoothly. Ask about any CEL history (R52 can throw codes for minor issues).
picks.bottomLine: Cheap fun with real personality — great if the supercharger and timing chain are healthy.

picks.whyGood: The E85 Z4 2.5i is a proper BMW roadster — inline-six, rear-wheel drive, electric folding top. At $9,499 with 88k miles and a 'Good Deal' rating, it's well-priced. 16 miles away in Deptford NJ. The 2.5i is the lighter, more balanced version (vs. the heavier 3.0i).
picks.whatToVerify: Test the power top through its complete cycle — the top mechanism is electric and can fail (replacement motors are available). Check for rocker panel rust (NJ salt). Listen for VANOS rattle on cold start. Inspect for coolant hose weeping (M54 engine has age-related hose failure). Verify the manual transmission shifts cleanly through all gears.
picks.bottomLine: Solid, stylish roadster at a good price — the electric top is the only real risk, test it thoroughly.

mustangs.introHtml: Five Mustang convertibles surfaced across Cars.com, AutoTrader, and CarGurus — ranging from Fox-body V8s to modern V6 S197s. Ranked GT trims first, then by value/proximity.

picks.whyGood: 44,000 original miles on a SN95 5.0 GT is almost unheard of. Last year of the 302 pushrod V8. Private seller, clean title, no accidents per Cars.com. Best combination of miles and price in the Mustang pool.
picks.whatToVerify: SN95 convertible top frame rots at the A-pillar-to-rocker junction — probe with a magnet. T-5 transmission: slow-shift to 3rd to check synchro. Replace fuel lines (age). Check rear axle for clunk on takeoff. Water staining on rear seat from top seal failure is common.
picks.bottomLine: The find of the Mustang bunch — 44k miles at this price is special if the frame is clean.

picks.whyGood: Last SN95 5.0 before the 4.6 modular swap in 1996. 55k miles, 'Great Deal' rating from GT Motors in Philadelphia. Nearly identical buy to the '94 but at a dealer with some accountability.
picks.whatToVerify: Same SN95 convertible frame rust checks as the '94. Verify 5-speed manual (1995 GT came with T-5 or 4-speed auto — you want the manual). Check 8.8 rear axle for clunk. Inspect the top frame at the rear window seam for rot.
picks.bottomLine: Another low-mileage SN95 GT — go manual if given the choice and have the frame inspected.

picks.whyGood: Fox-body 5.0 GT — the classic American muscle convertible. At 98k it's been driven but not abused. 16 miles away in Westville. Clean title, no accidents. Fox convertibles are increasingly collectible.
picks.whatToVerify: Fox convertible chassis flex is inherent — check for cowl shake and windshield frame cracks. Inspect the rear hatch/trunk area for water intrusion. T-5 third gear synchro test. Look for rust at the rear quarter panels and in the trunk floor. Verify the top bows are straight and the top seals.
picks.bottomLine: Fox-body collector appeal at a fair price — fun if mechanically sorted, expensive if not.

picks.whyGood: Only 9 miles away — closest Mustang in the search. The S197 V6 (3.7L) is actually capable (305hp) and far more reliable than the older GTs. At 96k it's mid-life. Fair Deal means it's priced at market.
picks.whatToVerify: S197 convertible: check the rear seat area for water staining from the top seal. Test the power top cycle. Verify the 6-speed manual shifts without grinding. Check for IRS clunk on equipped models (post-2011). Look at the rear axle for any differential whine.
picks.bottomLine: Closest pick, modern reliability — the V6 isn't as exciting but it won't leave you stranded.

picks.whyGood: Premium trim adds the 9-speaker Shaker audio and better interior. 79k miles, 'Good Deal' rating. Last year of the S197 before the S550 redesign — clean, refined drive.
picks.whatToVerify: Same S197 checklist as the 2012. At 79k this should have at least another 80k before major work if maintained. Verify all top seals — Delran NJ cars see salt. Check for any SiriusXM or MyFord Touch electronics issues (common on this era).
picks.bottomLine: Best-equipped Mustang in the bunch — Good Deal on a well-optioned last-year S197.
